title: Setup
body:
Start the MCP server in a separate terminal first:

```
Terminal 1:  make serve         # mcpkit-Go fixture on :3101 by default
Terminal 2:  make demo          # this walkthrough (--tui for interactive TUI)
```

Point the walkthrough at a different host via either of:

```
go run . --demo --url http://localhost:1234       # CLI flag
MCPKIT_SERVER_URL=http://localhost:1234 make demo # env var
```

Any MCP host can connect to the running server (Claude Desktop, VS Code, MCPJam, basic-host). The walkthrough below acts as a scripted host that issues the protocol calls directly through `*mcpkit/client.Client` — no LLM, no browser, no JS. The same protocol calls drive the iframe when you run `make demo-app EXAMPLE=cohort-heatmap` in basic-host (see the [centralized guide](../README.md#other-ways-to-test-a-fixture)).

output: ERROR: Post "http://localhost:3101/mcp": dial tcp [::1]:3101: connect: connection refused

title: What the App iframe does with all this
body:
This is the point where a Go-only scripted host hits its limit: everything from here runs in the iframe's JavaScript via the MCP Apps bridge, not over the MCP wire.

When `make demo-app EXAMPLE=cohort-heatmap` runs this fixture inside basic-host, the host:

1. Calls `tools/call get-cohort-data` exactly as step 3 above.
2. Reads `ui://get-cohort-data/mcp-app.html` exactly as step 4 above.
3. Renders the iframe in a sandbox, loads the JS bundle, and pushes the tool result into the iframe via the [mcp-app-bridge](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/ext-apps/blob/main/src/app-bridge.ts) `postMessage` channel.

Inside the iframe — which is what you saw in step 4's bytes — the App SDK (React in this fixture) does a **moderate** bridge dance:

- `useApp()` hook handles the bridge handshake; the iframe gets the initial tool result via the same path step 3 exercised.
- `app.getHostContext()` + `app.onhostcontextchanged` — pulls host-side context (theme, safe-area insets, current display mode) so the heatmap chrome adapts when basic-host changes its display mode or you switch dark/light.
- Two `<select>` filter dropdowns (Metric, PeriodType) drive a React effect that calls `app.callServerTool({name: "get-cohort-data", arguments: {metric, periodType, ...}})` whenever the user changes a filter. Every dropdown change is a fresh tools/call round-trip through the bridge.
- Result's `structuredContent` is cast to the `CohortData` shape (TS interface inferred from the same zod schema upstream's server uses) and the heatmap re-renders.

What this fixture **doesn't** do (notably):

- **No `app.registerTool`.** The iframe doesn't expose any tools to the model — it's a viewer driven by the model picking inputs, not a widget the model directly manipulates. Compare with `budget-allocator`, which registers 5 model-callable tools (`set-allocation`, `set-company-stage`, etc.).
- **No `app.updateModelContext`.** The user fiddling with the dropdowns doesn't push state back to the model context. Compare again with `budget-allocator`, which updates the model context after every slider change so the next-turn LLM prompt sees the current allocations.

cohort-heatmap sits between `quickstart` (bare-minimum bridge dance — just `ontoolresult` + a button calling `callServerTool`) and `budget-allocator` (rich bridge dance — `registerTool` ×5 + `updateModelContext`) on the App-ness spectrum: filter-driven re-fetches via `callServerTool` plus host-context awareness, but no model-callable tools and no model-context pushback.

title: Where to look in the code
body:
- `main.go` — the fixture. ~200 lines: one TypedAppTool + the data generator (port of upstream's `generateCohortData` with a PCG seed for visual stability).
- `walkthrough.go` — this file. Each step's curl + Go recipe is the canonical wire reproduction.
- `../README.md` — narrative + screenshots + the [Other ways to test a fixture](../README.md#other-ways-to-test-a-fixture) guide.
- Upstream's iframe source: `/tmp/ext-apps/examples/cohort-heatmap-server/src/mcp-app.tsx` — the React App that drives `useApp` + `callServerTool` + `getHostContext`.
